# All Lectures Should Be Recorded and Posted to the Class Website: An Analytical Dive into Possibly Making a Great Class Even Better 


## What do people think about having a recorded lecture?

A survey indicates that more students than not would find it at least somewhat helpful if lecture was recorded and posted to the class website. A whopping 358 students in favor outnumbers 176 students that are either against, or have no opinion, on this matter. 


<img src="/static/imgs/image-3.png" alt="Histogram of Lesson Video Ratings" width="500"/>


Furthermore, the number of individuals who think having some sort of pre-class video would be somewhat beneficial outweighs those opposed or unbothered with a vote of 388 to 146. 


<img src="/static/imgs/image-1.png" alt="Histogram of Pre-Lesson Video Ratings" width="500"/>


In addition to both of these, 393 students are in favor of a class livestream, while only 141 are not, or are unbothered. 


<img src="/static/imgs/image-2.png" alt="Histogram of Livestream Ratings" width="500"/>


These three survey questions demonstrate the student body's desire to have some sort of recorded content for class. 


## What do the specific population breakdowns look like?


As previously mentioned, the proportion of students that think recorded content would be helpful in some fashion make up the majority of the population of this class. However, some think it may be more beneficial than others. The following violin plots can show us, relatively, the population density of each ranking for each idea pertaining to recorded class content. The following graph represents the violin plot of student responses to the idea of recording lecture.


<img src="/static/imgs/image-4.png" alt="Violin Plot of Lesson Video Ratings" width="500"/>


While violin plots may help with relative vizualization of the proportion of the population that wants lecture recorded, an empirical cumulative distribution plot can show the numerical analysis of the histogram while conveying student proportion information like the violin plot. The following empirical cumulative distribution plot also represents the student repsonses to the idea of recording lecture.


<img src="/static/imgs/image-5.png" alt="Empirical Cumulative Distribution Plot of Lesson Video Ratings" width="500"/>


The same two analyses can be done when asked about pre-class videos and livestreams. The following two graphs pertain to the pre-class videos idea.


<img src="/static/imgs/image-6.png" alt="Violin Plot of Pre-Lecture Video Ratings" width="500"/>
<img src="/static/imgs/image-7.png" alt="Empirical Cumulative Distribution Plot of Pre-Lecture Video Ratings" width="500"/>


When asked about pre-recorded content, students felt similarly; they were in favor of it. The final two graphs of this section pertain to the livestream idea.


<img src="/static/imgs/image-8.png" alt="Violin Plot of Livestream Ratings" width="500"/>
<img src="/static/imgs/image-9.png" alt="Empirical Cumulative Distribution Plot of Livestream Ratings" width="500"/>


Similarly to lecture recordings and pre-recorded content, the livestream idea recieved mostly positive feedback.


## How necessary would recording content be from a student perspective?


Data shows that almost 80% of students find this class to be at least somewhat difficult. Of all the students, over 25% reported that they feel this class is very difficult. When 1 in every 4 students reports a class to be very difficult, it may not be a bad idea to provide supplemental instruction, or alternative methods for learning class content. 


## Conclusion


The data from the above analysis supports the conclusion that lecture should be recorded and posted to the class website. The histogram and violin plots show that more people than not would find the practice at least somewhat helpful. There are alternative options if this specific idea cannot be implemented. The analysis of opinions on pre-recorded content and class livestreams also show postive feedback, mostly. Some of these techniques are similar to what is done for the current course. Two class lectures have been posted, and 4 review session recordings have been posted. These videos have helped in my personal life, and I'm certain it has for others as well. Continuing this trend may prove beneficial in the future. 

An unintended negative consequence of these actions, if they were to take place, is the possibility that less students show up to lecture. Some may feel that going to class is not necessary if the content is going to be available regardless online, especially in a class that does not take attendance. Perhaps to mitigate this issue, there could be several small extra credit opportunities at random points throughout the semester that is only offered to those who attend lecture. This could incentivize lecture attendence, while maintaining the current structure of the course. 

If this unintended negative consequence were to take place, it could result in a tradeoff between availability of student resources and student grades. While the availability of student resources would increase with more access to recorded instruction, student grades might falter. This could happen as a result of students not showing up to class, and then performing poorly as a result.

While having some sort of recorded class content may lead to potential challenges for a students with a specific mindset, it should not be withheld from the entire student body. More students have responded positively to these ideas than neutral and negative combined. Furthermore, this class is percieved as difficult, currently, by many. The consequence of skipping class is the potential for suffering grades. If individuals do not feel that they need to go to class because the content is recorded, then they pose that risk to themselves and themselves only. The other students should not be punished for the actions of few. For these reasons, lecture should be recorded. If this is not possible, then perhaps other similar ideas like pre-recorded lessons or livestreams may be implemented.
